questions.question: Why is raw payload size usually not the same as real retained storage footprint?
options: Because replicas, indexes, and backups often add substantial overhead on top of the raw data.
Because payload size matters only for cache systems.
Because storage engines always compress data to zero.
Because retention windows do not affect disk use.
questions.explanation: Total footprint is usually much larger than raw data due to operational copies and indexing.

questions.question: What is the most important retention clarification to ask early?
options: Whether the interviewer prefers decimal or binary units.
How long data must remain queryable in hot, warm, or cold tiers.
Which IDE the team uses.
Whether users know the exact record size.
questions.explanation: Retention by tier changes cost and architecture decisions dramatically.

questions.question: If a service ingests about 450 GB of raw data per day and stores 3 replicas, what is rough daily durable size before indexes?
options: 150 GB
450 GB
questions.explanation: Three copies of 450 GB is about 1.35 TB.

questions.question: Which design choice most directly lowers long-term storage cost when old data is rarely queried?
options: Moving older data into cheaper warm or cold storage tiers.
Increasing the replication factor for all data.
Serving reads from more regions.
Using smaller load balancers.
questions.explanation: Tiering matches data temperature to storage cost.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of forgetting index overhead in a storage estimate?
options: The service may over-provision CPU but not disk.
The retained footprint can be materially undercounted, leading to poor capacity planning.
The system will automatically delete old records too early.
The architecture can no longer use caches.
questions.explanation: Indexes can add a large and persistent disk multiplier.

questions.question: Which observability metric best validates storage-growth assumptions after launch?
options: Daily bytes ingested and retained bytes by tier
Number of engineers on call
Average dashboard load time
Count of architecture review meetings
questions.explanation: Real ingest and retained footprint are the direct signals.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with aggressive cold-tiering?
options: Lower storage cost, but slower restore and query latency for older data.
Higher CPU efficiency, but zero savings on storage.
Better consistency, but lower availability only for hot data.
No downside as long as replication stays high.
questions.explanation: Tiering saves money by accepting slower access to colder data.

questions.question: When is a one-number total storage answer most misleading?
options: When the system mixes hot queryable data, warm history, and compliance archives with different access needs.
When there are exactly two servers.
When records are smaller than one kilobyte.
When the team uses managed infrastructure.
questions.explanation: Different data temperatures usually deserve different storage treatment.
